skill.name: longbridge
skill.description: Use Longbridge market data, brokerage account tools, SDK references, CLI commands, MCP setup, and AI documentation for stock research and trading workflows.
skill.summary: Access Longbridge market data, account analysis, trading APIs, and developer references.
skill.author: longbridge

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many command, network, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns, but review shows most are Markdown documentation examples or false positives from symbols and code fences. Confirmed concerns are legitimate but sensitive brokerage workflows: remote install scripts, OAuth token caches, account data access, and order placement APIs. No evidence found of malicious payloads, hidden exfiltration, or prompt-injection instructions that attempt to override the audit.

medium_findings.title: Remote Install Scripts Piped to Shell
medium_findings.description: The documentation recommends curl piped to sh and PowerShell iwr piped to iex for CLI installation and updates. This is a real supply-chain risk if a user runs the commands without verifying the installer, although the endpoints are Longbridge-owned documentation targets and not evidence of malware.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell patterns are explicit and confirmed in setup documentation. Confidence is not higher because the commands install an expected vendor CLI rather than an unexplained payload.

medium_findings.title: Brokerage Order Placement and Account Operations
medium_findings.description: The skill documents portfolio, assets, bank card, withdrawal, order placement, replacement, and cancellation workflows. These are expected brokerage capabilities, but AI-assisted use can cause financial harm if commands are executed without clear user confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files directly show account-data and order APIs, plus an explicit recommendation to confirm order placement. The risk is misuse of legitimate functionality, not confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: OAuth Token Cache and Sensitive Account Context
medium_findings.description: The references describe OAuth token caches under the user home directory and account or portfolio commands that can expose sensitive financial data. This is legitimate authentication behavior, but outputs and local files should be treated as sensitive.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple references describe token storage and account access. The risk is contextual data exposure because I did not find code that reads or exfiltrates those token files.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick and Weak-Crypto False Positives
low_findings.description: Many static hits labeled Ruby shell backticks or weak cryptography are Markdown code fences, inline command examples, stock symbols, market suffixes, or words such as HAS and SH. These do not represent executable Ruby or cryptographic code in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is visibly Markdown reference text and symbol tables. Static labels do not match the semantic context of the files.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import and Process Spawn False Positives
low_findings.description: The dynamic import and process-spawn findings map to ordinary language-specific examples such as Go import blocks and Rust tokio::spawn tasks. They do not start OS processes or dynamically load untrusted code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The snippets are normal SDK documentation patterns. I found no JavaScript dynamic import expression or command-spawning implementation in those locations.

low_findings.title: Expected Longbridge Network Endpoints
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to Longbridge documentation, MCP, OAuth, SDK docs, or product Markdown pages. Network access is central to this skill, but the reviewed endpoints match the stated market-data and brokerage purpose.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The domains and endpoints are consistent with Longbridge services described by the skill. I did not find evidence of unrelated third-party exfiltration endpoints.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe to Shell Installer Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: Installer examples pipe remote script output directly into a shell. This is a dangerous pattern even when used for legitimate vendor installation documentation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact shell pipeline patterns are present at the cited lines. The pattern is dangerous by execution model, independent of whether the vendor intent is legitimate.

content.value_statement: Investors and developers need reliable market data, account context, and API guidance in one workflow. This skill points Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to Longbridge CLI, SDK, MCP, and documentation patterns.
actual_capabilities: Explains Longbridge CLI commands for quotes, positions, portfolios, news, filings, fundamentals, and IPO data.
Guides OAuth setup for CLI, MCP, Python SDK, Rust SDK, and Go SDK workflows.
Documents SDK methods for quotes, subscriptions, account balances, positions, orders, and executions.
Shows when to use CLI, Python, Rust, Go, MCP, or Markdown documentation sources.
Supports US, Hong Kong, China A-share, Singapore, and crypto symbol formats.
Highlights MCP usage for AI tools that need live market or account data.
limitations: The skill is documentation only and does not include executable code files.
Live data requires Longbridge services, network access, and user authorization.
Trading and account features depend on approved OAuth scopes and brokerage permissions.
Financial outputs should not be treated as personalized investment advice.

use_cases.title: Research a public company
use_cases.description: Pull quotes, financial reports, analyst estimates, news, filings, and sentiment before preparing a market summary.

use_cases.title: Build a market data integration
use_cases.description: Choose the CLI or SDK, configure OAuth, and find quote, K-line, and content methods.

use_cases.title: Review account exposure
use_cases.description: Inspect positions, assets, margin details, executions, and cash flows before discussing portfolio risk.

prompt_templates.title: Check a quote
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Longbridge to check the latest quote for AAPL.US and summarize price, volume, and extended-hours data if available.

prompt_templates.title: Compare two stocks
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Longbridge data to compare TSLA.US and NVDA.US by price action, valuation rank, analyst estimates, and recent news.

prompt_templates.title: Review my portfolio
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Longbridge account tools to summarize my positions, cash, buying power, margin risk, and largest unrealized gains or losses.

prompt_templates.title: Design an SDK workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Python or Go workflow that authenticates with Longbridge OAuth, fetches quotes, reads filings, and monitors order updates.

output: A concise market brief with latest quote movement, intraday trend, relevant news, and filings.
A short note separating confirmed Longbridge data from interpretation.
A reminder that the result is informational, not personal financial advice.

output_examples.input: Show my Hong Kong portfolio risk.
output: A portfolio summary covering positions, cash, margin details, and concentration risk.
A list of items that may need user review before any trading action.
A confirmation request before placing or changing orders.

output_examples.input: How should I use the Go SDK for quotes and orders?
output: A workflow that selects OAuth setup, quote context creation, subscriptions, trade context creation, and order update monitoring.
References to the relevant SDK sections without copying raw source code.

best_practices: Ask the user to confirm every order placement, replacement, or cancellation before execution.
Use the smallest OAuth scope needed for the current task.
Use Longbridge CLI help or official SDK docs when command flags or method signatures may have changed.
anti_patterns: Do not treat market analysis as personalized investment advice.
Do not place trades from an ambiguous user request.
Do not expose OAuth tokens, account identifiers, or raw portfolio data in shared outputs.

faq.question: Does this skill execute trades by itself?
faq.answer: No. It documents Longbridge tools that can trade after user authorization. Users should confirm every trade action.

faq.question: Which markets are covered?
faq.answer: The skill documents US, Hong Kong, China Shanghai, China Shenzhen, Singapore, and crypto symbol formats.

faq.question: Can it access my portfolio?
faq.answer: Only if Longbridge account tools are connected and authorized. Portfolio data should be handled as sensitive information.

faq.question: Which programming languages are covered?
faq.answer: The references cover Python, Rust, and Go SDK usage, plus CLI and MCP workflows.

faq.question: Does it require API keys?
faq.answer: The preferred setup uses OAuth. Some SDK examples also mention environment-based credentials.

faq.question: Is this financial advice?
faq.answer: No. The skill helps retrieve and organize data. Users remain responsible for investment decisions.
